content.text: Evaluation data curation is the discipline of deliberately selecting, annotating, validating, and managing the datasets used to assess the behaviour of AI agents and models. Unlike training data — where scale and diversity are primary goals — evaluation data must be precisely representative of the scenarios the system will face in production, carefully annotated with ground-truth answers, free from contamination with training data, and maintained as a versioned, append-only registry that enables longitudinal comparison of model performance across releases.
What It Is

An evaluation dataset is a curated collection of inputs, expected outputs, and associated metadata (difficulty, category, domain) used to measure a model or agent's performance on a defined set of quality axes. For agentic systems, the evaluation dataset extends beyond input-output pairs to include multi-step trajectories: a task specification, the expected sequence of tool calls and intermediate reasoning steps, and the expected final output. Evaluation data curation is the process of building and maintaining this dataset with the rigour required to make evaluation results meaningful and comparable over time.

Curation encompasses five activities: requirement elicitation (defining what capabilities, safety properties, and alignment behaviours the evaluation should measure), collection (gathering seed examples from production logs, expert elicitation, and adversarial generation), annotation (assigning ground-truth labels, ratings, or reference answers with documented inter-annotator agreement), validation (checking that examples are unambiguous, correct, and non-contaminated), and registry management (versioning the dataset, controlling access, and tracking which model versions were evaluated against which dataset versions).
Why It Matters
Evaluation quality determines whether improvements are real:

A model that scores better on a poorly curated evaluation set may have simply memorised the evaluation examples or exploited annotation artifacts. Rigorous evaluation data is the foundation of trustworthy model comparison.
Safety evaluations require adversarial examples:

Evaluating an agent's safety properties requires examples that specifically probe for unsafe behaviours — harmful content generation, prompt injection susceptibility, PII disclosure. These cannot be collected passively; they require deliberate adversarial curation.
Regression testing requires a stable golden set:
A curated golden evaluation set — examples that the previous model version passed — is the primary mechanism for detecting regressions when a new model version or fine-tuning run is deployed.
Contamination invalidates results:

If evaluation examples appear in the training data, the model has seen them before and its performance on them is inflated. Systematic contamination detection is necessary for evaluation results to be valid.
Longitudinal progress requires versioned datasets:

If the evaluation dataset changes between runs, performance trends cannot be attributed to model improvements versus dataset changes. Versioned, immutable evaluation datasets are required for meaningful longitudinal comparison.
Benefits
Reliable capability measurement:

A carefully curated evaluation dataset provides a reliable signal of whether a model has the capability it claims, covering the full distribution of inputs it will face in production, not just the easy cases.
Regression safety net:
The golden evaluation set catches regressions before deployment, reducing the risk that a model update that improves performance on one capability degrades another.
Regulatory compliance evidence:
For EU AI Act high-risk AI systems, documented evaluation results from a curated evaluation set constitute the testing evidence required in the technical documentation (Annex IV).
Adversarial robustness assurance:
Deliberately curated adversarial examples provide evidence that the agent's safety controls have been systematically tested against known attack patterns.
Comparative benchmarking:
A shared, versioned evaluation dataset enables fair comparison between different model versions, fine-tuning strategies, and retrieval configurations.
Trade-offs and Challenges
Expert annotation is expensive:
High-stakes evaluation requires subject-matter expert annotators. Medical, legal, and financial evaluation examples may cost $50 to $200 per annotated example for expert annotation with validation.
Evaluation sets become stale:

As the product evolves — new capabilities, new use cases, new geographic markets — the evaluation set must be updated to remain representative. Maintaining the set is an ongoing investment, not a one-time project.
Contamination detection is probabilistic:

Detecting whether a training dataset contains an evaluation example requires exact or near-duplicate matching across potentially billions of training tokens. Exact hash matching catches verbatim duplicates but misses paraphrases; semantic similarity matching is expensive at scale.
Annotation disagreement on subjective tasks:

For tasks where quality is subjective (writing quality, helpfulness, tone), different annotators give different ratings. Evaluation of subjective qualities requires careful rubric design, pilot rounds, and calibration training for annotators.
Test set leakage through fine-tuning:
If developers have access to the evaluation set, they may — even unintentionally — make fine-tuning decisions that overfit to it. Access to the evaluation set must be strictly controlled.
How It Works

Evaluation data curation begins with a requirements workshop. The product team, safety team, and evaluation team jointly define the evaluation axes — capability dimensions (reasoning, instruction following, tool use, multi-step planning), safety dimensions (refusal on harmful prompts, resistance to prompt injection, PII non-disclosure), and alignment dimensions (preference following, helpfulness, honesty). For each axis, the team defines the target metric, the acceptable threshold, and the minimum number of examples required to measure the metric with statistical confidence.

Collection assembles examples from three sources. Production logs (suitably anonymised) provide real-world inputs that reflect the actual distribution of tasks users bring to the agent. Expert elicitation produces high-quality examples in rare or high-stakes scenarios that would be under-represented in production logs — a medical diagnosis agent may have very few examples of rare disease presentations in production logs, but medical experts can write them. Adversarial generation produces examples designed to probe for failure modes — red team prompts, edge cases, and examples that have historically caused problems for similar systems.

Annotation assigns each example a reference answer or a set of acceptable answers, a difficulty rating, a category label, and a rubric-based quality score if the task is subjective. For tasks with a deterministic correct answer (factual question answering, code that must pass unit tests), a single expert annotator is sufficient if the answer can be independently verified. For tasks with a subjective correct answer (writing quality, helpfulness), three to five annotators rate each example, and the rating is aggregated using the mean or median after outliers are removed.

Contamination checking runs at the end of the annotation phase and after any modification to the training dataset. The check computes n-gram fingerprints of all evaluation examples and compares them against the training dataset using a MinHash locality-sensitive hashing scheme. Any evaluation example with a Jaccard similarity above a threshold (typically 0.8) against a training example is flagged for review and, if confirmed as contaminated, removed from the evaluation set and replaced with a new example.
Architecture and Design

attrs.alt: Evaluation Data Curation Pipeline showing requirement, collection, annotation, validation, and registry stages with evaluation suite taxonomy and contamination prevention architecture

content.text: The evaluation registry is the central data store. It holds each evaluation example with its metadata (category, difficulty, axes, annotator IDs, annotation scores, inter-annotator agreement, contamination check status) and each evaluation run result (model version, dataset version, score per axis, per-example pass/fail, timestamp). The registry's append-only design ensures that historical evaluation results are never overwritten — essential for longitudinal tracking.

The contamination barrier enforces strict isolation between the training dataset store and the evaluation dataset store. The two stores exist in separate storage accounts with separate access credentials. The ML pipeline's training path has no access to the evaluation store, and the evaluation harness has read-only access to a quarantined copy of the training data hash index (not the training data itself) for the purposes of contamination checking.

The evaluation harness is a service that accepts a model identifier (or model endpoint URL), a dataset version, and a set of evaluation axes, and returns per-axis scores and per-example pass/fail details. The harness enforces that the requested dataset version exists in the registry with a contamination-clean status before running any evaluation. Results are written to the registry with the model identifier, dataset version, and harness version, enabling full reproducibility.
When to Use It
Before any model or agent release, to measure performance against defined capability, safety, and alignment thresholds.
After any fine-tuning run, to detect regressions on the golden evaluation set before the fine-tuned model is promoted to production.
When a new capability is added to the agent, to add evaluation examples for that capability and establish a baseline.
When red team testing identifies a new failure mode, to add adversarial examples for that failure mode to the evaluation set.
When the agent is extended to a new geography or domain, to add evaluation examples representative of the new context.
Periodically (e.g., quarterly) to refresh examples that may have become stale due to world knowledge cutoff drift.
How to Implement
Define evaluation axes and per-axis success thresholds with input from the product, safety, and legal teams.
Collect seed examples from production logs (anonymised), expert elicitation, and adversarial generation. Target at least 200 examples per axis for statistical power.
Annotate examples with ground-truth answers and metadata. Use three annotators for subjective tasks; compute and record inter-annotator agreement (target Cohen's kappa >= 0.7).
Run contamination checks against the current training dataset hash index. Remove or replace flagged examples.
Register the validated, contamination-clean examples in the evaluation registry under a new version identifier (e.g., eval-suite-v3.1.0).
Configure the evaluation harness to run the suite as part of the CI/CD pipeline for every model training run.
Set up regression alerts: if any axis score drops by more than a defined delta from the previous run, block promotion and alert the team.

# Evaluation data registry client
from dataclasses import dataclass, field
from typing import Optional
import hashlib, json, time

@dataclass
class EvalExample:
    example_id: str
    category: str          # e.g., "tool_use", "safety_refusal", "reasoning"
    difficulty: str        # "easy", "medium", "hard", "adversarial"
    prompt: str
    reference_answer: str
    acceptable_answers: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    axes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    iaa_score: float = 0.0  # inter-annotator agreement
    contamination_status: str = "pending"  # pending / clean / contaminated
    annotator_ids: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

def compute_example_fingerprint(prompt: str, k: int = 5) -> set:
    """Generate k-gram fingerprint for contamination checking."""
    tokens = prompt.lower().split()
    return set(
        " ".join(tokens[i:i+k])
        for i in range(len(tokens) - k + 1)
    )

def check_contamination(eval_example: EvalExample, training_fingerprints: set, threshold: float = 0.8) -> bool:
    """Return True if the example is contaminated (too similar to training data)."""
    eval_fp = compute_example_fingerprint(eval_example.prompt)
    if not eval_fp:
        return False
    intersection = eval_fp & training_fingerprints
    jaccard = len(intersection) / len(eval_fp | training_fingerprints - intersection)
    return jaccard >= threshold

class EvalRegistry:
    def __init__(self, db_conn):
        self.db = db_conn
        self._training_fingerprints: Optional[set] = None

    def load_training_fingerprints(self, training_hash_index_path: str):
        """Load pre-computed training fingerprints for contamination checking."""
        with open(training_hash_index_path) as f:
            self._training_fingerprints = set(json.load(f))

    def register_example(self, example: EvalExample, suite_version: str) -> str:
        if self._training_fingerprints is None:
            raise RuntimeError("Training fingerprints not loaded — cannot check contamination")

        is_contaminated = check_contamination(example, self._training_fingerprints)
        example.contamination_status = "contaminated" if is_contaminated else "clean"

        if is_contaminated:
            raise ValueError(f"Example {example.example_id} is contaminated — cannot register")

        # Persist to registry (pseudo-code)
        record = {
            "example_id": example.example_id,
            "suite_version": suite_version,
            "category": example.category,
            "contamination_status": example.contamination_status,
            "iaa_score": example.iaa_score,
            "registered_at": time.time(),
        }
        # self.db.insert("eval_examples", record)
        return example.example_id
Examples

// Evaluation suite example record
{
  "example_id": "tool-use-0042",
  "suite_version": "eval-suite-v3.1.0",
  "category": "tool_use",
  "difficulty": "medium",
  "axes": ["tool_use_accuracy", "planning"],
  "prompt": "You have access to a calendar tool and a weather API. Book a tennis court for this coming Saturday if the weather forecast shows no rain between 10am and 12pm.",
  "reference_answer": "The agent must: (1) call weather_api(date=this_saturday, time_range=10:00-12:00), (2) check result for precipitation > 0, (3) if no rain: call calendar_tool.book(facility='tennis_court', date=this_saturday, start=10:00, end=12:00); (4) if rain: report back that booking was not made due to weather.",
  "acceptable_answers": [],
  "evaluation_type": "trajectory",
  "expected_tool_calls": ["weather_api", "calendar_tool.book"],
  "iaa_score": 0.91,
  "annotators": ["expert-001", "expert-004"],
  "contamination_status": "clean",
  "added_in_version": "eval-suite-v3.0.0",
  "last_verified": "2026-06-01"
}
Real-World Example

A customer service AI company builds an agent that handles returns, refunds, and product enquiries. The evaluation team defines three axes: task completion (did the agent resolve the customer's issue correctly?), policy compliance (did the agent follow the returns policy as documented?), and tone (was the response appropriately empathetic and professional?).

For task completion and policy compliance, the team uses a deterministic evaluation — each example has a correct answer that can be verified by a rule (e.g., 'a return requested within 30 days of purchase is approved; beyond 30 days it is rejected unless the product is defective'). The annotation team of two subject-matter experts independently annotates 500 examples; inter-annotator agreement is 0.88 (Cohen's kappa), above the 0.80 target.

For tone, the team uses a 5-point Likert scale with a rubric covering empathy, clarity, and professionalism. Five annotators rate each of 200 examples. The mean rating is used as the reference score. Inter-annotator agreement (Krippendorff's alpha) is 0.72 — acceptable but at the lower boundary; the team revises the rubric and runs a second calibration round before finalising.

Before registering the suite, the team runs contamination checks against the agent's fine-tuning dataset (10,000 customer service transcripts). Three evaluation examples are flagged as near-duplicates of fine-tuning examples; they are removed and replaced with new examples from a different customer cohort. The final suite version (eval-suite-cs-v1.2.0) is registered in the evaluation registry and integrated into the CI/CD pipeline. Every time a new agent version is released, the suite runs automatically. The team sets a regression threshold of 2 percentage points on task completion; any release that drops task completion by more than 2pp is blocked and must go through a root-cause investigation before promotion.
Common Mistakes
Not checking for contamination:

The most common evaluation quality failure. Even accidental contamination — when production examples sampled for evaluation overlap with the fine-tuning data — inflates scores and renders the evaluation meaningless.
Building evaluation sets from the easy case distribution:
If examples are sampled from production without stratification, easy cases dominate. The model scores 95% on the evaluation set but fails catastrophically on the 5% of hard cases that matter most.
Updating the evaluation set without versioning:
If examples are modified or removed between evaluation runs without creating a new version, historical scores cannot be compared to current scores.
Using the same team to build training and evaluation data:
The training data team's knowledge of what examples they used may unconsciously influence their evaluation example selection, producing contamination that is hard to detect.
Treating evaluation pass rate as sufficient:
An aggregate pass rate hides per-category failures. An agent that scores 90% overall but 40% on safety refusal examples has a serious safety problem obscured by a flattering aggregate score.
Not including adversarial examples:
A safety evaluation that only tests normal usage does not measure safety. Adversarial examples designed to elicit unsafe behaviour are mandatory for any agent that handles sensitive domains.
Best Practices
Use stratified sampling:
Sample evaluation examples proportionally from all categories and difficulty levels, including a deliberate oversample of adversarial and edge-case examples relative to their production frequency.
Set inter-annotator agreement targets before annotation begins:
Define the minimum acceptable kappa or alpha before running annotation, and do not accept the final dataset until the target is met. Post-hoc adjustment of thresholds invalidates the quality claim.
Enforce strict access control on the evaluation set:

Only the evaluation harness service account and authorised evaluation leads should have read access. No developer who works on training or fine-tuning should have access to the raw evaluation examples.
Refresh adversarial examples quarterly:
Red team findings, vulnerability disclosures, and user-reported failure modes should be incorporated into the evaluation set on a quarterly cycle to ensure coverage of newly discovered risk patterns.
Record why examples were removed:
When contaminated or low-quality examples are removed, record the reason. The removal audit trail is part of the dataset provenance that a regulator may request.
Run the evaluation harness against the baseline model before each release:
Include the previous production model in every evaluation run to provide a direct comparison. Without a baseline, a score of 92% is meaningless; with a baseline of 91%, it is evidence of improvement.
Performance and Scalability

Evaluation at scale requires the harness to be parallelisable. Each example is independent, so the harness can distribute evaluation across multiple workers — a batch of 1,000 examples evaluated by a model with a 1-second per-example latency takes 17 minutes on a single worker but 2 minutes on 8 workers. For models accessed via API (inference endpoints), the harness should implement rate limit handling, exponential back-off, and result caching so that a partial run due to an API outage can be resumed rather than restarted from scratch.

Contamination checking at scale uses MinHash locality-sensitive hashing to avoid O(n*m) comparisons between the evaluation set and the training set. The training dataset is pre-indexed into a MinHash table with 128 hash functions. Each evaluation example is hashed and compared against this table. The comparison for a single evaluation example takes microseconds, making contamination checking for a 1,000-example evaluation set against a billion-token training set feasible in under a minute.
Tooling and Ecosystem

HELM (Holistic Evaluation of Language Models) from Stanford provides a standardised evaluation framework with a registry of evaluation scenarios and a harness for running models against them. EleutherAI's Language Model Evaluation Harness (lm-eval) is widely used for open benchmarks. For custom evaluation data, a PostgreSQL or DynamoDB table combined with a Python evaluation harness is sufficient for most enterprise deployments. Label Studio and Argilla are annotation platforms that record annotator metadata and compute inter-annotator agreement statistics as part of the annotation workflow.
Comparison with Alternatives
Approach
Strengths
Limitations
Public benchmark suites (MMLU, HumanEval)
Zero curation cost; established baselines
Not representative of production distribution; contamination risk from pretraining; does not cover safety/alignment axes
Automated LLM-as-judge evaluation
Scalable; covers subjective tasks; cheap
Judge model itself has biases; may not detect subtle safety failures; not auditable for regulatory purposes
A/B testing (production deployment)
Real user signal; no annotation required
Requires production deployment; risky for safety evaluation; slow feedback loop
Custom curated evaluation registry (this topic)
Representative; contamination-free; versioned; auditable
High annotation cost; ongoing maintenance; requires contamination check infrastructure
Security and Reliability

The evaluation set must be treated as a security-critical asset. If an adversary can read the evaluation set before training, they can construct a fine-tuning dataset that teaches the model to perform well on the evaluation examples without generalising, producing a model that appears aligned but is not. Evaluation set access must be controlled via separate credentials from the training pipeline, with all accesses logged. The evaluation harness must run in an isolated environment that does not have write access to the evaluation registry, preventing a compromised harness from modifying historical evaluation results.
Mental Model

Think of the evaluation suite as the final examination that a model must pass before graduating to production. Like a well-designed university exam, the evaluation suite must: cover the full syllabus of capabilities (not just the easy questions), include some questions that are deliberately difficult or adversarial, be set and held by examiners who are independent of the instructors (preventing coaching to the test), and be kept secret from students until the exam starts (contamination prevention). And like an exam, the results are recorded permanently and compared against the results of all previous cohorts to track whether standards are rising or falling over time.
Key Takeaways
Evaluation data curation is the discipline of building and maintaining the datasets that measure whether an AI agent actually behaves as intended.
Contamination prevention — ensuring training data does not contain evaluation examples — is the single most important technical control in evaluation data governance.
Inter-annotator agreement (Cohen's kappa >= 0.7 for objective tasks; Krippendorff's alpha >= 0.7 for subjective tasks) is the primary quality metric for annotation quality.
Evaluation suites must be versioned and append-only; removing or modifying examples without versioning destroys the ability to compare scores across model releases.
Adversarial examples must be deliberately curated into the evaluation set — passive collection from production will under-represent edge cases and failure modes.
The evaluation harness should be integrated into CI/CD, with regression thresholds that block promotion if performance drops beyond a defined delta.
